title: New York Fed Releases Reports Focused on the Impact of Recessions on Schools and 'Regular and Predictable' Treasury Bill Issuance

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed released two Economic Policy Review articles: one comparing Great Recession impacts on NY and NJ school finances, and another examining whether 'regular and predictable' Treasury bill issuance minimizes financing costs. The Treasury research found the additional cost of adhering to regular/predictable issuance is likely less than one basis point.
key_points: The Treasury article examines cost differences between cost-minimization auction strategies versus regular/predictable issuance strategies
Authors include Paul Glasserman (Columbia University) and Allen Zhang (former deputy director of Treasury's Office of Debt Management)
The estimated additional cost of regular and predictable Treasury issuance is less than one basis point
The school finance article compares New York (maintained funding/spending) versus New Jersey (downward funding shifts, instructional spending cuts) during the Great Recession
Both articles were scheduled for a forthcoming edition of the Economic Policy Review
why_it_matters: The Treasury research provides empirical evidence that the U.S. approach of maintaining regular, predictable auction schedules imposes minimal additional financing cost (less than 1 bp), supporting the policy rationale for orderly debt management practices.
market_structure_context: The article discusses Treasury auction strategy research regarding the trade-off between cost-minimization and issuance predictability. While the research touches on Treasury market structure (auction methodology), it is historical academic research from 2017 with no direct implications for current market operations.
important_numbers: Less than 1 basis point (estimated additional cost of regular/predictable issuance)

issues_found: Source is a press release announcing research, not the actual research papers themselves (thin, low-signal source)
Only one of two articles mentioned has tangential market structure relevance; the school finance article is entirely outside scope
Source age (January 2017) is nearly 9.5 years old with no clear current market structure application
Research findings about <1bp auction cost have limited archival value for current market operations
market_structure_context acknowledges 'historical academic research from 2017 with no direct implications for current market operations'

human_review.notes: Historical press release about academic research. Relevance to current market structure is minimal. One of two articles (school finance) is entirely outside the topic scope. The Treasury article discusses research findings from 2017, not current market structure. Flagged as low priority for archive inclusion given age and indirect relevance.

notes: Historical record (2017). This is a press release announcing two Economic Policy Review research articles. Only the second article on Treasury bill issuance has tangential relevance to market structure. The first article on school finances is outside scope entirely. Research conclusions about issuance regularity (<1 bp cost) are stale and do not inform current market structure dynamics. Consider deprioritizing for archive given age and indirect relevance.
